title: A hybrid IFENN solver for generalizable modeling of phase-field fracture initiation and propagation
authors: Panos Pantidis, Fouad Amin, Diab Abueidda, Mostafa E. Mobasher
abstract: In this paper we demonstrate how the Integrated Finite Element Neural Network (IFENN) framework can effectively model the entire evolution of phase-field fracture, including the initiation and propagation stage, across generalizable geometries. IFENN is a hybrid scheme for coupled computational mechanics problems, tightly coupling a standard FEM solver (mechanical equilibrium) with a pre-trained neural network (coupled field). In this work, the phase-field diffusion equation is approximated with: i) a DeepONet architecture with Kolmogorov-Arnold networks in the trunk and branch (DeepOKAN) for the initiation stage, and ii) a Convolution Neural Network (CNN) for the propagation stage. Both networks are trained only once, on a benchmark geometry, using a purely physics-informed approach based on the maximum strain energy and the phase-field variable. The training process utilizes an extremely small number of training increments and only a limited number of Gauss points that are strategically sampled from the fracture process zone. These features enable a substantial decrease of the offline training cost. To address the extrapolation of the DeepOKAN predictions in regions away from the crack tip during the inference stage, we implement a set of artificial boundary conditions to enforce the near-zero values in the far-field predictions. We showcase the flexibility and numerical accuracy of the proposed methodology across both the training and unseen geometries.

infographic.key_insight: IFENN combines a standard FEM solver with a pre-trained neural operator to model both initiation and propagation of phase-field fracture across new geometries.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The approach offers a generalizable and efficient pathway for fracture prediction with reduced offline training cost, making it attractive for computational solid mechanics workflows.
method: FEM handles mechanical equilibrium; a neural network approximates the phase-field diffusion equation.
DeepOKAN is used for crack initiation, while a CNN is used for crack propagation.
Training is physics-informed, using a benchmark geometry, sparse increments, and limited Gauss points from the fracture process zone.
infographic.evidence_signal: The model is trained once on a benchmark case and then evaluated on both training and unseen geometries, with artificial boundary conditions added to improve far-field predictions.
infographic.watch_item: Accuracy may depend on carefully sampled fracture-process-zone data and on artificial boundary conditions to control extrapolation away from the crack tip.
infographic.paper_title: A hybrid IFENN solver for generalizable modeling of phase-field fracture initiation and propagation

title: One-Step Flow Matching for Generative Modeling of Path-Dependent Physical Fields
authors: Yijing Zhou, Jasmin Jelovica
abstract: Physical simulations for intricate geometries with path-dependent constitutive models face difficulties due to the enormous computational cost they require. Recently, the emergence of generative AI models, which succeed in image and video synthesis tasks, has provided a promise to further improve simulations. Although U-Net-based denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) have been adopted for elastic stress field generation, they typically require hundreds of sampling steps, and applications of generative models to path-dependent, e.g. plastic, stress fields remain very limited. In this work, we propose a novel flow matching (FM) model based on a transformer backbone for high-resolution path-dependent stress field generation with stochastic loading-unloading paths and geometry. The proposed model operates within the latent space of a variational autoencoder (VAE) and formulates the simulation of plastic fields as a video synthesis task, directly generating the stress fields across all time steps. Meanwhile, we design a non-Gaussian source distribution for flow matching, such that crossings among conditional transport paths are reduced during training. This enables our model to generate satisfactory samples in one step without relying on distillation. In addition, we introduce token-level loading embeddings and two auxiliary networks to further enhance the model performance in path-dependent simulation. The results demonstrate that, even with a limited training dataset, our model can accurately generate high-resolution path-dependent fields. It is much more computationally efficient than finite element analysis, providing a speedup of 6 to 7 times over FEM on CPUs and approximately two orders of magnitude speedup on consumer-grade GPUs.

infographic.key_insight: The paper shows that a transformer-based flow matching model in VAE latent space can generate high-resolution, path-dependent plastic stress fields in a single step.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a much faster surrogate than finite element analysis for geometry- and loading-history-dependent solid mechanics simulations, with substantial CPU and GPU speedups.
method: Models plastic field evolution as video synthesis over all time steps.
Uses flow matching with a non-Gaussian source distribution to reduce path crossings and enable one-step sampling.
Adds token-level loading embeddings and two auxiliary networks to improve path-dependent prediction.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports accurate generation even with limited training data and runtime gains of about 6 to 7 times over FEM on CPUs and roughly two orders of magnitude on consumer GPUs.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on the chosen VAE latent representation and training coverage of loading paths, so unseen path histories or geometries could still be challenging.
infographic.paper_title: One-Step Flow Matching for Generative Modeling of Path-Dependent Physical Fields

title: From Weights to Features: SAE-Guided Activation Regularization for LLM Continual Learning
authors: Evan Ning, Wei Xue, Dong Lou, Yike Guo
abstract: Weight-space regularization methods such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) are the standard approach to catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. However, those methods tend to underperform when applied to large language models. We argue that such underperformance can be partly explained by the ``polysemantic'' nature of large language models: per-weight importance estimates utilized by EWC-style regularization are too coarse and cannot isolate the knowledge that needs protection. In this paper, we propose regularizing instead in the model's activation space, using pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as a monosemantic feature dictionary. From the perspective of constrained optimization, we derive a new loss function that uses the SAE feature dictionary to explicitly balance stability and plasticity, and show that EWC is a special case in the one-sided weight-space penalty setting. Unlike replay-based methods that store or revisit examples from earlier tasks, our method requires no previous-task data after mask construction: current-task data is used to compute a compact SAE feature mask, and only this mask is retained for later training. Further, since the feature space has significantly lower dimensionality than the parameter space, the proposed method is more memory efficient. On the TRACE and MedCL continual learning benchmarks, the method achieves the strongest result among approaches without introducing task-specific architectural components, also surpassing traditional weight-space regularization methods like EWC. Beyond performance comparisons, we provide empirical evidence for the polysemanticity thesis: task-relevant representations are linearly separable in the SAE feature basis but indistinguishable from chance in the weight basis, and weight-space protection is nearly non-selective at the concept level.

infographic.key_insight: Instead of protecting LLM weights directly, the paper protects monosemantic SAE features to better preserve prior knowledge during continual learning.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This suggests a more selective and memory-efficient way to reduce catastrophic forgetting in large models, which is important for deploying adaptive AI systems in evolving engineering workflows.
method: Use a pretrained Sparse Autoencoder to map activations into a sparse, interpretable feature space.
Build a compact mask from current-task data and regularize only the protected SAE features during later training.
Frame the approach as constrained optimization, with EWC emerging as a special case of one-sided weight-space protection.
infographic.evidence_signal: On TRACE and MedCL, the method outperformed prior non-task-specific continual learning approaches, including traditional weight-space regularization such as EWC.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on having a useful pretrained SAE feature dictionary and a reliable mask construction step, which may add setup complexity and sensitivity to representation quality.
infographic.paper_title: From Weights to Features: SAE-Guided Activation Regularization for LLM Continual Learning

title: Mapping Political-Elite Networks in Europe with a Multilingual Joint Entity-Relation Extraction Pipeline
authors: Kirill Solovev, Jana Lasser
abstract: Whether political elites organise into rent-seeking coalitions that capture public resources or civic networks that sustain governance is a central question in comparative politics. Yet observing these complex, informal, and adversarial ties at scale has historically required intensive manual coding, while automated text-as-data methods have largely been limited to simple co-occurrence. Recent large language model (LLM) approaches offer a path forward but often rely on proprietary APIs, lack cross-lingual capability, and struggle with scalable entity resolution. We present a modular, fully open-weight pipeline for multilingual joint entity-relation extraction that builds signed, temporal knowledge graphs from massive unstructured news corpora. It combines span-based named-entity recognition (NER) with a three-stage linking cascade mapping mentions to language-independent Wikidata identifiers; a high-throughput, ontology-constrained mixture-of-experts model then uses guided decoding to extract directed, signed relationships grounded in a domain ontology. A full-coverage spot-check against a 3491-relation gold standard shows high textual correctness (68.2% strict to 93.7% lenient). Two large-scale case studies validate the pipeline against the public record. In Austria, it reconstructs a political party's complete lifecycle, dating internal fractures and tracking personnel into successor factions and court convictions. In a Polish corpus, it uncovers the overlapping economic and governance networks of state-enterprise patronage, alongside the structurally balanced, signed conflict network of the polarized Civic Platform (Platforma Obywatelska, PO)--Law and Justice (Prawo i Sprawiedliwość, PiS) duopoly. By bridging raw multilingual text and structured relational data, our framework provides a robust, replicable foundation for cross-national empirical computational social science.

infographic.key_insight: The paper introduces a fully open, multilingual pipeline that converts large news archives into signed, temporal political-elite knowledge graphs by jointly extracting entities and relations across languages.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It enables scalable, reproducible information extraction for building structured datasets from unstructured text, reducing manual coding effort and supporting cross-national computational social science.
method: Span-based multilingual NER identifies person and organization mentions.
A three-stage linking cascade resolves mentions to Wikidata IDs across languages.
An ontology-constrained mixture-of-experts model with guided decoding extracts directed, signed relations.
infographic.evidence_signal: Against a 3,491-relation gold standard, the pipeline achieved 68.2% strict and 93.7% lenient textual correctness, and case studies in Austria and Poland matched public-record events.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on robust entity resolution and ontology coverage, so linking errors or domain-specific relation types can propagate through the downstream knowledge graph.
infographic.paper_title: Mapping Political-Elite Networks in Europe with a Multilingual Joint Entity-Relation Extraction Pipeline

title: Can Scale Save Us From Plasticity Loss in Large Language Models?
authors: J. Fernando Hernandez-Garcia, Tomás Figliolia, Beren Millidge
abstract: The loss of plasticity - the ability of a network to learn new information after having already learned older information - is a fundamental challenge in creating artificial neural networks capable of continual learning. Although this phenomenon has been known for decades, it has mostly been studied in older, relatively small architectures and rarely in natural-language domains. To determine whether loss of plasticity remains a problem in the modern transformer-based LLM paradigm, we study plasticity loss in GPT-style Transformer models trained on a multilingual continual learning problem. Consistent with prior work, we find evidence of plasticity loss across models ranging from 5M to 314M non-embedding parameters, as measured by deterioration on a held-out Vietnamese probing task. We further find that the onset of plasticity loss follows a predictable scaling law, growing sublinearly with model size. These results suggest that larger models may delay the measurable effects of plasticity loss, but that increasing parameter count alone is likely to be insufficient to completely prevent it. We also find evidence of plasticity loss under stationary multilingual training, challenging the view that the phenomenon is exclusive to continual learning with abrupt task changes. Overall, our results suggest that even large Transformer language models trained on natural-language will eventually lose the ability to efficiently adapt to new data after sufficiently long training, in both continual and stationary settings.

infographic.key_insight: Scaling up GPT-style language models delays but does not eliminate plasticity loss: even large models eventually become less able to learn new information.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For continual-learning systems, parameter scaling alone is not a reliable fix, so model design must also preserve adaptability over long training horizons.
method: Trained multilingual GPT-style Transformer models with 5M to 314M non-embedding parameters.
Evaluated plasticity using a held-out Vietnamese probing task during continual learning.
Also tested stationary multilingual training to see whether plasticity loss appears without abrupt task switches.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a measurable deterioration on the held-out Vietnamese probe across model sizes, with the onset of plasticity loss following a sublinear scaling law.
infographic.watch_item: Larger models may postpone the problem, but the study suggests they do not fully prevent plasticity loss, so long-run adaptability remains a concern.
infographic.paper_title: Can Scale Save Us From Plasticity Loss in Large Language Models?

title: Droplet Fusion as a Relaxation Process: Comparison with Shape Recovery of Newtonian and Viscoelastic Droplets
authors: Mohammad Moein Naderi, Zhangli Peng, Huan-Xiang Zhou
abstract: Biomolecular condensates formed by phase separation often exhibit viscoelastic behavior, yet their shape recovery and fusion dynamics are frequently interpreted using purely viscous models. Here, we develop a unified theoretical and computational framework to quantify how viscoelasticity governs these two processes. We combine analytical theory for small-deformation shape recovery with axisymmetric finite-element simulations based on the Oldroyd-B constitutive model to systematically investigate both shape recovery and droplet fusion under comparable conditions. Our results show that, although both processes are driven by capillary forces, they are fundamentally distinct in their underlying physics. Shape recovery is governed by global viscocapillary relaxation of a single connected interface and follows single- or multi-exponential decay depending on the relative magnitude of the viscocapillary timescale and the stress relaxation time. In contrast, droplet fusion is intrinsically a multistage process involving localized curvature-driven neck formation, rapid bridge expansion, and a transition to global relaxation. We demonstrate that viscoelasticity introduces an additional intrinsic timescale that governs the competition between capillary driving and stress relaxation, characterized by the Deborah number. This leads to enhanced intermediate-stage fusion dynamics and modified relaxation behavior compared to Newtonian droplets. Furthermore, we show that the presence of an exterior fluid introduces additional hydrodynamic dissipation, significantly slowing the fusion process. Finally, we compare the computationally predicted droplet fusion in the Newtonian and viscoelastic cases with a stretched-exponential empirical formula. Deviations observed in viscoelastic regimes highlight the limitations of purely viscous descriptions and the need for models incorporating stress relaxation.

infographic.key_insight: Droplet fusion and droplet shape recovery both arise from capillarity, but viscoelastic droplets relax through an additional stress-relaxation timescale that makes fusion a multistage process distinct from simple shape recovery.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For modeling biomolecular condensates and soft materials, the results show that purely viscous laws can mispredict relaxation rates and that constitutive viscoelasticity is needed to capture fusion dynamics and dissipation.
method: Analytical small-deformation theory for shape recovery
Axisymmetric finite-element simulations using the Oldroyd-B model
Comparison of Newtonian and viscoelastic droplets under matched conditions
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports systematic theory-simulation comparisons showing single- or multi-exponential recovery for shape relaxation and deviations from stretched-exponential fusion behavior in viscoelastic regimes, with exterior fluid increasing hydrodynamic dissipation.
infographic.watch_item: Oldroyd-B is a simplified constitutive model, so quantitative predictions may depend on how well a real condensate matches its single-relaxation-time assumptions.
infographic.paper_title: Droplet Fusion as a Relaxation Process: Comparison with Shape Recovery of Newtonian and Viscoelastic Droplets

title: Mechanical response of quasi-two-dimensional colloidal clusters under uniaxial tension
authors: Yanhui Yang, Jiawei Kang, Yao Li, Xiaoguang Ma
abstract: Despite extensive studies of equilibrium conformations of colloidal clusters, little is known about their mechanical response. Here, we investigate the tensile behavior of a quasi-two-dimensional colloidal cluster subjected to uniaxial tension up to fracture. The sample is a ribbon-shaped assembly of 16 colloidal beads bound by short-range depletion attraction. Using multiple optical tweezers, we clamp the cluster at both ends and perform a tensile test along its long axis. Combining video microscopy with particle tracking, we measure the tensile stress, strain, and particle configurations during deformation. We observe diverse mechanical response behaviors, including elastic, plastic, and soft-mode deformation, with fracture occurring at a strain near 10\%. To explain these behaviors, we construct a spring-mass frame model with breakable elastic bonds. We perform canonical Monte Carlo simulations on the full model with 32 degrees of freedom and compute the statistical distributions of mechanical observables using a simplified model with only 7 degrees of freedom. Both the simulations and the theoretical calculations accurately reproduce the experimental stress--strain curves. Moreover, the configuration distributions predicted by the simplified model agree well with both experiment and simulation in the elastic and soft-mode regimes, with only minor discrepancies in the plastic regime. This work demonstrates that the simplified spring-mass model captures the essential physics governing the rich tensile response behavior of the colloidal cluster.

infographic.key_insight: A quasi-2D colloidal cluster under uniaxial tension shows elastic, plastic, and soft-mode deformation before fracturing at about 10% strain, and a simplified spring-mass model captures these behaviors.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The study provides a mechanics framework for predicting how small bonded particle assemblies deform and fail, which is useful for designing tunable soft materials and mesoscale structures.
method: Clamp a 16-bead ribbon-shaped colloidal cluster with multiple optical tweezers and stretch it along its long axis.
Use video microscopy and particle tracking to measure stress, strain, and evolving configurations during loading.
Model the cluster with breakable spring-mass bonds; validate with Monte Carlo simulations and a reduced 7-DOF theory.
infographic.evidence_signal: Experiment, full-model simulation, and simplified-theory predictions all reproduce the stress-strain response, with configuration distributions matching well in elastic and soft-mode regimes and only minor mismatch in plastic deformation.
infographic.watch_item: The reduced model is less accurate in the plastic regime, so quantitative predictions near rearrangement or damage events may need the full model or additional state variables.
infographic.paper_title: Mechanical response of quasi-two-dimensional colloidal clusters under uniaxial tension

title: Attention mechanism for scalable mesh-based neural surrogates of free-surface fluids
authors: Federico Lanteri, Massimiliano Cremonesi
abstract: High-fidelity simulations of free-surface flows using Lagrangian methods such as the Particle Finite Element Method (PFEM) are computationally demanding due to continuous domain updates and repeated solution of the governing equations. This challenge is further amplified by non-Newtonian rheologies, where material nonlinearities increase computational cost. These limitations motivate the development of efficient surrogate models to approximate PFEM dynamics at reduced cost. While data-driven deep learning approaches are promising, a key challenge is designing models that operate on arbitrary and evolving geometries. We propose a self-attention-based neural surrogate for PFEM simulations of free-surface flows. The architecture leverages attention mechanisms to model node interactions and capture complex spatial dependencies, while preserving the PFEM mesh discretization. This provides a geometric and topological framework for remeshing and node redistribution, maintaining high-quality spatial discretization during rollouts, improving long-term stability, and enabling reconstruction of derived mechanical quantities via standard finite element operators. Two attention formulations are considered: a standard self-attention mechanism and a linear variant that reduces computational cost and improves scalability. The models are evaluated on two- and three-dimensional free-surface flow benchmarks with evolving geometries, varying material parameters, and non-Newtonian fluids. Results show accurate prediction of transient dynamics and final configurations, with significantly improved scalability. The mesh-based formulation also enables direct reconstruction of quantities such as stress fields. Overall, the framework provides an accurate and scalable surrogate strategy for PFEM simulations in engineering-scale applications.

infographic.key_insight: A self-attention neural surrogate can learn PFEM free-surface fluid dynamics directly on evolving meshes, enabling accurate long-horizon prediction while preserving geometric fidelity.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a scalable reduced-order alternative to expensive free-surface CFD simulations, including non-Newtonian flows and postprocessed stress reconstruction for engineering analysis.
method: Mesh-preserving self-attention on PFEM nodes to model spatial interactions
Two variants: standard self-attention and a cheaper linear attention version
Supports remeshing/node redistribution during rollout to maintain mesh quality
infographic.evidence_signal: Evaluated on 2D and 3D free-surface benchmarks with evolving geometry and varying material parameters, showing accurate transient and final-state predictions with improved scalability.
infographic.watch_item: Performance still depends on robust mesh evolution and training data coverage, so instability may appear when extrapolating to unseen geometries or rheologies.
infographic.paper_title: Attention mechanism for scalable mesh-based neural surrogates of free-surface fluids

title: Interpretable Concept-Guided Polynomial Tabular Kolmogorov-Arnold Network for EEG-Based Mild Cognitive Impairment Detection
authors: Yosef Bernardus Wirian, Qiang Cheng
abstract: Early and scalable detection of mild cognitive impairment (MCI) remains an unresolved clinical challenge. Existing EEG-based screening approaches are constrained by handcrafted feature pipelines that discard neurophysiologically meaningful domain structure and deep learning classifiers that sacrifice interpretability for performance. No existing work unifies physiologically organized concept encoders, cross-concept interaction modeling, and nonlinear tabular classification in a sleep EEG-based MCI detection framework. This study proposes Concept-guided Polynomial-transformed Tabular learning using Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (CPTabKAN), which maps heterogeneous EEG-derived features into domain-informed concept representations, expands them via degree-2 polynomial transformation to expose first- and second-order interactions, and applies a Fourier-parameterized TabKAN classifier to learn nonlinear decision boundaries. CPTabKAN was evaluated on the Study of Osteoporotic Fractures cohort (372 subjects, overnight polysomnography), using 1,379 features organized into ten physiologically motivated concept groups. Under 10-fold cross-validation, CPTabKAN-Second Order achieved a weighted F1-score of 0.9038 (SD 0.034), outperforming GradientBoosting by 5.65 percentage points (t(9)=1.934,p=0.043, one-sided paired test), with advantages persisting under SMOTE-based balancing. Ablation analysis confirmed independent contributions from each component. Concept importance analysis revealed that power spectral density, multi-scale entropy, and Hjorth parameters dominated first-order weights, while cross-concept interactions involving Lempel-Ziv-Welch complexity, statistics, demographics, and slow oscillations exceeded all first-order scores. These results demonstrate that concept-structured, interaction-aware tabular learning surfaces physiologically coherent reasoning, supporting clinical trust.

infographic.key_insight: CPTabKAN combines physiologically grouped EEG concepts, polynomial interaction expansion, and a Kolmogorov-Arnold-style classifier to improve interpretable MCI detection.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The method offers a clinically oriented, scalable tabular learning pipeline that preserves domain structure while exposing which EEG concepts and cross-concept interactions drive decisions.
method: Organize 1,379 EEG-derived features into 10 physiologically motivated concept groups.
Apply degree-2 polynomial transformation to capture first- and second-order concept interactions.
Use a Fourier-parameterized TabKAN classifier for nonlinear prediction and concept importance analysis.
infographic.evidence_signal: On the Study of Osteoporotic Fractures cohort (372 subjects), CPTabKAN-Second Order reached a weighted F1-score of 0.9038 in 10-fold cross-validation, outperforming GradientBoosting by 5.65 percentage points.
infographic.watch_item: Results depend on careful concept grouping and cross-validation design, and the added interaction modeling may be sensitive to class imbalance and cohort-specific feature distributions.
infographic.paper_title: Interpretable Concept-Guided Polynomial Tabular Kolmogorov-Arnold Network for EEG-Based Mild Cognitive Impairment Detection

title: Direct stress imaging from shear wave propagation
authors: Yiwei Duan, Michel Destrade, Wenchang Tan, Guo-Yang Li
abstract: Quantitative imaging of stress fields in heterogeneous solids remains challenging because stress is not directly measurable and is typically inferred from deformation using constitutive models. Here we present Acoustoelastic Imaging (AEI), a non-destructive framework for reconstructing stress fields from shear wave propagation. AEI exploits the acoustoelastic effect, whereby pre-existing stress modifies local wave dynamics, and formulates stress recovery as an inverse problem of the governing wave equations. Using full shear waveform inversion with physics-informed learning, AEI reconstructs wave-equation coefficients from full-field wave measurements, enabling estimation of stress magnitude and principal directions without explicit constitutive model specification or material-parameter calibration. We demonstrate sub-wavelength spatial resolution (< 0.28 λ) and accurate reconstruction of nonuniform stress fields in heterogeneous materials through numerical simulations and ultrasound shear wave elastography experiments. These results establish a general framework for high-resolution stress imaging and provide a route toward non-invasive mapping of internal mechanical states in complex materials and biological tissues.

infographic.key_insight: Acoustoelastic Imaging (AEI) reconstructs internal stress fields directly from shear-wave measurements by inverting the wave physics, avoiding explicit constitutive modeling.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables non-destructive, high-resolution stress mapping in heterogeneous solids and biological tissues, useful for diagnostics, structural assessment, and materials characterization.
method: Measure full-field shear wave propagation in the stressed material.
Use physics-informed full waveform inversion to recover wave-equation coefficients.
Infer stress magnitude and principal directions from the recovered coefficients without prior material calibration.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical simulations and ultrasound shear wave elastography experiments showing accurate stress-field reconstruction with sub-wavelength spatial resolution below 0.28λ.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on measurement quality, wave-model fidelity, and the ability to separate stress effects from heterogeneity or other material uncertainties.
infographic.paper_title: Direct stress imaging from shear wave propagation

title: Primary damage and mechanical degradation of WTaCrV refractory high-entropy alloy: effects of solid-solution and chemical ordering
authors: Yihan Wu, Pengfei Yu, Yaohong Suo, Lei Zhang
abstract: As advanced nuclear reactors demand novel irradiation-tolerant materials, this study investigates the radiation damage and mechanical degradation of the promising WTaCrV refractory high-entropy alloy (RHEA). To isolate complex nanoscale chemical effects, we propose an atomistic modeling strategy comparing Average-Atom (AA), random solid-solution (RSS), and local chemical order (LCO) configurations using newly developed interatomic potentials. Collision cascades simulations reveal that the number of Frenkel pairs follow NRSS > NLCO > NAA at the same radiation dose. While the RSS effect accelerates defect generation due to rugged energy landscapes, LCO enhances lattice cohesion to mitigate radiation damage. Despite more primary defects in the RSS and LCO configurations compared with the AA configurations, the RSS and LCO effects can suppress radiation-induced mechanical degradation. Irradiation severely degrade the homogenized AA model but exert a limited impact on the strength and flow stress of the RSS and LCO models. This exceptional resistance is driven by inherent lattice distortion resulting from interactions among different alloy elements, which outweighs point defect induced lattice disruptions. Moreover, the complex interactions between deformation twins and point defects cause confined plastic flow, elevating flow stress in the RSS and LCO models. The findings provide atomistic guidance for performance assessment of next-generation structural materials for extreme nuclear environments.

infographic.key_insight: Chemical disorder and local chemical order in WTaCrV create lattice distortion that can offset radiation damage and preserve strength better than a homogenized average-atom model.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For irradiation-tolerant reactor materials, the study suggests that controlling nanoscale chemical structure may be as important as composition for maintaining mechanical integrity under extreme damage.
method: Atomistic simulations of collision cascades in WTaCrV using newly developed interatomic potentials
Comparison of Average-Atom (AA), random solid-solution (RSS), and local chemical order (LCO) configurations
Evaluation of Frenkel pair production and post-irradiation mechanical response
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports that Frenkel pair generation follows NRSS > NLCO > NAA at the same dose, while irradiation severely degrades AA but has limited impact on strength and flow stress in RSS and LCO.
infographic.watch_item: The conclusions depend on atomistic modeling choices and newly developed potentials, so transferability to experimental microstructures and reactor operating conditions should be validated.
infographic.paper_title: Primary damage and mechanical degradation of WTaCrV refractory high-entropy alloy: effects of solid-solution and chemical ordering

title: Mesh-RL: Coupled subgrid reinforcement learning
authors: Behnam Gheshlaghi, Bahador Rashidi, Shahin Atakishiyev
abstract: Reinforcement learning in large or sparse-reward environments suffers from slow temporal-difference reward propagation, as value information spreads only locally across the state space. We propose Mesh-RL, a spatial domain-decomposition framework inspired by the finite element method and domain decomposition theory, which partitions the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforces boundary-consistent temporal-difference updates. Such an approach enables localized learning while ensuring globally coherent value propagation. Unlike hierarchical or model-based approaches, Mesh-RL accelerates long-range credit assignment without modifying the reward function, Bellman operator, or introducing explicit planning mechanisms. We evaluate Mesh-RL on hazard-dense grid-world environments with varying geometries and mesh resolutions. Across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q, Mesh-RL consistently improves convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability. Higher mesh resolutions sustain exploration, prevent premature convergence, and substantially accelerate value propagation to distant states. While Dyna-Q already benefits from internal planning, it still achieves additional gains under structured decomposition. Overall, Mesh-RL introduces a principled spatial domain-decomposition mechanism for accelerating temporal-difference learning. Our framework bridges finite element method-inspired boundary-consistency techniques from scientific computing with reinforcement learning to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments. We will release source code of the study.

infographic.key_insight: Mesh-RL speeds up sparse-reward reinforcement learning by decomposing the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforcing boundary-consistent value updates for faster long-range credit assignment.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The method borrows finite-element/domain-decomposition ideas to improve sample efficiency and convergence stability in complex state spaces, which is useful for computational engineering optimization and control problems.
method: Partition the environment into overlapping mesh subgrids.
Apply boundary-consistent temporal-difference updates across subgrid interfaces.
Use the decomposition with Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q without changing rewards or Bellman updates.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports tests on hazard-dense grid worlds with different geometries and mesh resolutions, where Mesh-RL improved convergence speed, cumulative reward, and stability across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on choosing an appropriate mesh resolution and overlap; overly coarse or poorly matched decompositions may reduce the benefit or add implementation complexity.
infographic.paper_title: Mesh-RL: Coupled subgrid reinforcement learning

title: Hydrogen segregation around a straight screw dislocation in bcc iron
authors: Margot Lucas, Marie Landeiro Dos Reis, Sylvain Queyreau, Xavier Feaugas
abstract: The interaction between hydrogen and screw dislocations in bcc iron is central to understanding hydrogen embrittlement. A major challenge lies in the high-dimensional parametric landscape governing this interaction. In this work, we perform a comprehensive set of molecular simulations using a reliable neural network interatomic potential, systematically exploring hydrogen binding across dislocation core structures (easy and hard cores), site types, and concentrations. From these energetics, we construct a thermodynamic framework that quantifies the statistical relevance of the various trapping configurations, thereby significantly reducing the complexity of the problem. Our results show good agreement with the limited density functional theory data available in the literature. We further delineate the validity domain of an elastic dipole description of hydrogen-dislocation interactions, providing a simplified yet physically grounded modeling approach. Finally, we demonstrate that the easy-core configuration plays a key role in rationalizing experimental hydrogen solubility limits. These findings establish a consistent multiscale foundation for incorporating hydrogen-dislocation interactions into larger-scale models of plasticity and embrittlement.

infographic.key_insight: A thermodynamic, multiscale framework shows that hydrogen trapping around a straight screw dislocation in bcc iron is dominated by a few statistically relevant configurations, especially the easy-core state.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The results provide a physically grounded way to model hydrogen-dislocation interactions in iron, improving embrittlement and plasticity predictions for larger-scale constitutive simulations.
method: Molecular simulations with a neural-network interatomic potential
Systematic exploration of hydrogen binding across easy/hard dislocation cores, site types, and concentrations
Thermodynamic ranking of trapping configurations and comparison to an elastic dipole description
infographic.evidence_signal: The framework is validated against the limited density functional theory data available in the literature and used to rationalize experimental hydrogen solubility limits.
infographic.watch_item: The simplified elastic dipole model is only valid within a limited interaction regime and may miss core-specific behavior outside that domain.
infographic.paper_title: Hydrogen segregation around a straight screw dislocation in bcc iron

title: Certified Reachable Sets for Nonlinear Reaction--Diffusion Systems
authors: Mohamed Amine Ouchdiri, Mohamed Maghenem, Saad Benjelloun, Adnane Saoud
abstract: Reachability analysis for dynamical systems seeks to compute a set containing all reachable states at a given time. Compared to ordinary differential equations (ODEs), the analysis of nonlinear reaction--diffusion PDEs with parametric uncertainties remains largely underexplored, due to the infinite-dimensional state space and the variety of solutions under different parameters. We address this through a three-step procedure: 1) Finite Element Methods (FEM)s to discretise the space and generate a finite-dimensional FEM-based model, 2) Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) to build a Reduced-Order Model (ROM), and 3) set-based reachability-analysis methods applied to the ROM. We propose a framework that enables us to derive explicit upper bounds on the approximation errors introduced at each stage of the pipeline. In particular, we quantify the discrepancy between trajectories of the original PDE and those of the FEM-based discretization, as well as the error between the FEM-based model and the reduced-order model. Importantly, these bounds are shown to hold uniformly over the considered set of parameters. By combining these error estimates, we obtain an over-approximation of the reachable set of the original PDE. The approach is illustrated on the Allen--Cahn equation and a logistic growth PDE.

infographic.key_insight: The paper builds certified over-approximations of reachable sets for nonlinear reaction--diffusion PDEs by combining FEM discretization, POD reduction, and reachability analysis with explicit error bounds at each step.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives engineers a practical, parameter-uniform way to verify safety and uncertainty envelopes for PDE-based models without solving the full infinite-dimensional system.
method: Discretize the PDE with finite elements to obtain a finite-dimensional model.
Apply POD to construct a reduced-order model with bounded approximation error.
Run set-based reachability on the ROM and propagate error bounds to certify the original PDE reachable set.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports explicit upper bounds for FEM and ROM approximation errors, and demonstrates the framework on the Allen--Cahn equation and a logistic growth PDE.
infographic.watch_item: The certification depends on the quality of the FEM and POD approximations, so coarse meshes or too aggressive reduction may weaken the reachable-set bounds.
infographic.paper_title: Certified Reachable Sets for Nonlinear Reaction--Diffusion Systems

title: Parallel Manifold Steering: Efficient Adaptation of Large Associative Memories via Residual Energy Shaping
authors: Kanishk Awadhiya
abstract: Large Transformer models function as Dense Associative Memories (DAMs), retrieving knowledge via high-dimensional attractor dynamics driven by the self-attention mechanism \citep{ramsauer2020hopfield, wu2024attention}. However, adapting these frozen memory systems to new tasks presents a fundamental ``Plasticity-Stability'' dilemma. Current methods either risk catastrophic interference by modifying synaptic weights directly (e.g., LoRA) \citep{hu2021lora} or degrade associative capacity by clogging the retrieval buffer with static prompt tokens (e.g., VPT) \citep{jia2022vpt}. In this work, we propose \textbf{H-Res} (Hierarchical Residual Steering), a mechanism that modulates the effective energy landscape of the Transformer without altering its global equilibrium or expanding its sequence length. By formulating adaptation as a control problem on the activation manifold \citep{chen2018neuralode}, H-Res learns a state-dependent vector field that steers token trajectories into task-specific basins of attraction. We formally prove that H-Res preserves the attention entropy of the foundation model and facilitates Neural Collapse \citep{papyan2020prevalence}. Empirically, Manifold Steering outperforms global weight modification by 26\% on associative retrieval tasks and eliminates the computational overhead of prompt-based methods, scaling effectively to structured domains \citep{zha2023vtab}.

infographic.key_insight: H-Res adapts frozen Transformer associative memories by steering activations through a learned residual field, rather than changing weights or adding prompt tokens.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a more stable and efficient way to transfer large pretrained models to new tasks while preserving their retrieval capacity and avoiding sequence-length overhead.
method: Treats adaptation as control on the activation manifold.
Learns a state-dependent residual vector field to steer token trajectories toward task-specific attractors.
Preserves the foundation model’s global equilibrium and attention entropy.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a 26% improvement over global weight modification on associative retrieval tasks and claims effective scaling to structured domains.
infographic.watch_item: The method may require careful training and verification to ensure the learned steering field generalizes without destabilizing downstream behavior.
infographic.paper_title: Parallel Manifold Steering: Efficient Adaptation of Large Associative Memories via Residual Energy Shaping

title: Error estimates for $A$-stable backward difference full discretizations of Willmore flow of closed surfaces
authors: Nils Bullerjahn, Balázs Kovács
abstract: A proof of optimal-order $H^1$-norm error estimates is given for $A$-stable backward difference full discretizations (of order 1 and 2) of Willmore flow for closed two-dimensional surfaces. The numerical method discretizes a coupled system of evolution equations by evolving surface finite elements of polynomial degree at least two in space and backward difference method of order 1 or 2 in time. The convergence analysis is based on a stability analysis, based on energy estimates exploiting the anti-symmetric structure of the second-order system, in combination with Dahlquist's $G$-stability and the multiplier techniques of Nevanlinna and Odeh, with a new upper bound in the spirit of Dahlquist. Numerical experiments illustrate and complement the theoretical results.

infographic.key_insight: The paper proves optimal-order H1 error estimates for first- and second-order A-stable backward difference full discretizations of Willmore flow on closed surfaces.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It gives rigorous convergence guarantees for high-order evolving-surface FEM methods used to simulate curvature-driven surface evolution with controlled accuracy.
method: Evolve a coupled second-order surface PDE system with finite elements of degree at least two in space.
Apply backward difference time stepping of order 1 or 2 with A-stability.
Use energy estimates plus Dahlquist G-stability and Nevanlinna-Odeh multiplier techniques for the proof.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract states that numerical experiments are used to illustrate and complement the theoretical error estimates.
infographic.watch_item: The analysis assumes polynomial degree at least two and relies on a specialized stability framework, so implementation must preserve the coupled antisymmetric structure carefully.
infographic.paper_title: Error estimates for $A$-stable backward difference full discretizations of Willmore flow of closed surfaces

title: Analysis and approximation of a two-dimensional induction heating problem
authors: Gabriel R. Barrenechea, Katherine MacKenzie, Abner Salgado
abstract: In this paper, we analyse the existence of solutions and finite element approximation of a steady-state two-dimensional induction heating problem. One of the main difficulties of the problem is its right-hand side which, at a first sight, is only integrable. Using a priori regularity results for the PDEs involved it is shown that the natural weak formulation of the problem can be justified. Then, we study the finite element approximation and prove that the standard Galerkin FEM converges in convex domains and under suitable conditions on the mesh. We improve on this result by applying the recently-proposed bound-preserving method (BPM) to the heat equation, and show that this method converges to a solution of the problem under less stringent conditions on the domain and the mesh. As these analyses are carried out without any assumption on regularity of the solutions, then the convergence of the finite element method also proves existence of solutions. Several numerical experiments confirm the theoretical results, and showcase the improvement provided by the use of the bound-preserving method over the standard finite element method.

infographic.key_insight: The paper shows that a 2D steady-state induction heating model can be made mathematically well-posed and accurately approximated with finite elements, even when the heat source is only weakly integrable.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives engineers a reliable FEM basis for predicting induction heating behavior in coupled electromagnetic-thermal simulations, with improved robustness from a bound-preserving heat solver.
method: Derive a natural weak formulation using a priori regularity results for the coupled PDEs.
Prove convergence of the standard Galerkin FEM under convex-domain and mesh-quality assumptions.
Apply a bound-preserving method (BPM) to the heat equation to obtain convergence under weaker domain/mesh conditions.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports several numerical experiments that confirm the theory and demonstrate better performance for the bound-preserving method than standard FEM.
infographic.watch_item: The strongest convergence guarantees still depend on domain shape and mesh conditions, so practical implementations must control mesh quality and boundary geometry carefully.
infographic.paper_title: Analysis and approximation of a two-dimensional induction heating problem

title: A matrix-free, differentiable PyTorch solver for phase-field fracture: Formulation, benchmarks, and inverse analysis
authors: Allamaprabhu Ani, Jean-François Molinari, Ghatu Subhash, Sathiskumar Anusuya Ponnusami
abstract: A matrix-free, open-source PyTorch solver is presented for phase-field fracture on central processing units (CPUs) and graphics processing units (GPUs) without custom compiled extensions. In the explicit dynamic pathway, finite-element operations are formulated as element-wise tensor contractions with scatter-based accumulation, removing global sparse mechanics-stiffness assembly from the core time-stepping loop. Both Ambrosio-Tortorelli regularisations (AT1 and AT2), multiple energy decompositions (spectral, volumetric-deviatoric, and star-convex), and plane strain or plane stress assumptions are supported. The explicit mechanics kernels are compatible with PyTorch's automatic differentiation engine (autograd), while the implicit, bound-constrained damage solve is wrapped in a custom backward rule. This rule implements implicit differentiation through the conjugate-gradient (CG) linear solve and keeps memory independent of the internal CG iteration count. The same implementation runs unmodified across macOS, Linux, and Windows, and has been run on meshes of order $10^6$ nodes on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU. The solver is compared against four dynamic fracture cases (straight crack propagation, shear-induced kinking, dynamic branching, and crack-hole interaction in perforated plates) and two quasi-static cases (single-edge notched tension and a notched-holed plate). As a differentiability demonstration, the scalar fracture energy $G_c$ is recovered from observed crack patterns using PyTorch gradients through the forward solve and limited-memory Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (L-BFGS) optimisation. Recovery of $G_c$ with relative error below $10^{-3}$ is achieved after three accepted L-BFGS states for glass and two for alumina. The implementation can be extended and combined with differentiable optimisation and machine-learning components.

infographic.key_insight: The paper shows that phase-field fracture can be solved end-to-end in PyTorch without custom compiled code by using matrix-free tensor operations and differentiable implicit solves.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This makes large-scale fracture simulation portable across CPUs and GPUs while enabling gradient-based parameter identification and integration with optimization or machine-learning workflows.
method: Matrix-free finite-element mechanics using element-wise tensor contractions and scatter accumulation in PyTorch
Support for AT1/AT2 fracture regularizations, multiple energy splits, and plane strain/plane stress options
Autograd-compatible explicit dynamics plus a custom backward rule for the bound-constrained implicit damage solve via conjugate gradient
infographic.evidence_signal: The solver is benchmarked on four dynamic and two quasi-static fracture cases and demonstrated on meshes of about 10^6 nodes on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, including recovery of fracture energy Gc with relative error below 10^-3.
infographic.watch_item: The implicit damage solve relies on a custom backward/CG-based differentiation path, so performance and accuracy may depend on solver settings and convergence behavior.
infographic.paper_title: A matrix-free, differentiable PyTorch solver for phase-field fracture: Formulation, benchmarks, and inverse analysis

title: High performance construction materials fracture and high cycle fatigue assessment based on accelerated PF-CZM
authors: Jiaqi Li, Zhihua Xiong, Xuyao Liu, Hongyu Liu
abstract: Given the widespread application of high-performance materials in cyclic loaded infrastructure, a thorough understanding of the fracture and high-cycle fatigue behavior of high-performance materials is of critical importance. However, these behaviors predominantly rely on experimental methods, which are often costly, time-consuming and limited in generalizability. To address these issues, this paper proposes the constitution and high-cycle fatigue of Ultra-High Performance Concrete (UHPC) and High Strength Steel (HSS) within the Phase-Field Cohesive Zone Model (PF-CZM) framework. A generic stress-based failure criterion for UHPC is derived from the biaxial tensile test results to calculate crack driving force. Furthermore, a fatigue degradation function and an acceleration algorithm are integrated into the PF-CZM framework to enable efficient high-cycle fatigue simulations. In the acceleration algorithm, the envelope load is used to approximate the real cyclic load to avoid the simulation of each cycle, and the three-stage fatigue process is simulated through adjusting the cyclic increment adaptively. The proposed model successfully captures mixed-mode fracture and high-cycle fatigue behavior of UHPC and HSS, with validation provided through relevant experimental comparisons.

infographic.key_insight: The paper accelerates phase-field cohesive-zone fracture modeling to predict mixed-mode fracture and high-cycle fatigue in UHPC and HSS without simulating every load cycle.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It provides a more practical computational tool for assessing durability and failure risk in cyclic-loaded infrastructure made from high-performance materials.
method: Derive a generic stress-based failure criterion for UHPC from biaxial tensile tests.
Embed a fatigue degradation law into the PF-CZM fracture framework.
Use an envelope-load approximation plus adaptive cyclic increments to accelerate high-cycle fatigue simulation.
infographic.evidence_signal: The model is validated against relevant experimental comparisons and reproduces mixed-mode fracture and high-cycle fatigue behavior for both UHPC and HSS.
infographic.watch_item: The acceleration and adaptive-cycle assumptions may need careful calibration to ensure accuracy across different loading histories and material systems.
infographic.paper_title: High performance construction materials fracture and high cycle fatigue assessment based on accelerated PF-CZM

title: Anomalous Hall viscosity of altermagnets
authors: Iksu Jang, Rui Aquino, Jörg Schmalian, Rafael M. Fernandes
abstract: We show that the phonon Hall viscosity at zero magnetic field is a natural probe of altermagnetism. First, we demonstrate that the finite elements of the Hall viscosity tensor unambiguously distinguish altermagnets from ferromagnets and conventional antiferromagnets. We then microscopically compute the Hall viscosity in models for d-wave and g-wave altermagnets, and find a strong sensitivity to electronic spectrum features such as gapped Dirac points and Lifshitz transitions. This sensitivity reflects a strain-space Berry curvature monopole, which contrast to the multipolar character of the standard momentum-space Berry curvature in altermagnets. Since the Hall viscosity can be probed experimentally through magneto-acoustic measurements, it provides a compelling method to probe the broken symmetries and topology of insulating altermagnets.

infographic.key_insight: Phonon Hall viscosity at zero magnetic field provides a symmetry-sensitive fingerprint that distinguishes altermagnets from ferromagnets and conventional antiferromagnets.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The result suggests a magneto-acoustic route to detect broken symmetries and topological features in insulating altermagnets through strain–phonon response measurements.
method: Derive the Hall viscosity tensor response for altermagnetic systems at zero magnetic field.
Compute viscosity microscopically for d-wave and g-wave altermagnet models.
Link strong viscosity features to gapped Dirac points, Lifshitz transitions, and strain-space Berry curvature monopoles.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports analytical and microscopic model calculations showing that specific Hall-viscosity tensor components uniquely identify altermagnets and are highly sensitive to electronic structure changes.
infographic.watch_item: Experimental extraction may be challenging because the signal depends on subtle tensor components and may require clean insulating samples with well-resolved magneto-acoustic measurements.
infographic.paper_title: Anomalous Hall viscosity of altermagnets
